emergent_themes.description: Subscriptions create psychological permission to order more frequently, even when users recognize they might not be 'saving' money overall
Students expect delivery app prices to match in-store prices and view markup as unfair rather than accepting it as a service premium
Actual eating patterns are heavily social (restaurants with friends) despite claims of individual cooking habits
Restaurant availability on specific platforms drives switching behavior more than brand loyalty

coding_summary: P011 presents a fascinating case of say-do gaps and sophisticated subscription understanding. He claims to cook 50% of meals and order delivery 2-3 times weekly, but diary data reveals predominantly social restaurant dining with friends and only 1 delivery order in 7 days. His relationship with delivery is highly pragmatic - he actively price compares across platforms, understands subscription economics deeply, and switches based on deals and restaurant availability. Key themes include 'laziness' as a socially acceptable narrative for convenience-seeking, subscription memberships creating ordering permission, and strong expectations for price transparency and student-specific value. His post-graduation projections suggest delivery will increase as campus social dining decreases.
coverage_gaps: No significant coverage gaps - all ROs received primary codes. RO4 has fewer segments as expected for a current undergraduate, but post-graduation expectations were captured.
